hero.title:
What is DevOps,
really?
hero.subtitle: (beyond the buzzword.)
hero.lead: A scribbled-in field guide to DevOps — what it is, what it isn't, and the handful of ideas you actually need to be useful on day one.
hero.note: ← drag the thumb tabs on the right →

welcomeStickies.html:
✎ Warning:

"DevOps" is one of those words companies print on job postings and nobody fully agrees on. We're going for the practical core.

✎ A promise:

no "transform your culture" slides. Pipelines, environments, observability — the parts you actually wire up.

levels.label: BASIC
levels.intro: — the two ideas that turn 'pile of code' into 'a thing in production'.

sticky.html: Imagine a factory. You drop your changes at one end. At the other end, a finished, tested, deployed thing rolls out — every single time, the same way.

blocks.html:
That's CI/CD. Continuous Integration tests every change. Continuous Delivery (or Deployment) ships it. The point isn't the tools — it's that the steps between "I wrote it" and "it's live" are automatic and repeatable.

The traditional alternative: one senior engineer with a runbook in a Google Doc that hasn't been updated since 2019.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: assembly line — code commit on the left, deployed app on the right

blocks.label: THE PIPELINE
blocks.caption: if any step fails, the belt stops. nobody pretends the change is fine.

bad.label: WITHOUT CI/CD
bad.html:
"Friday afternoon deploy."

Someone SSHes in. Pulls. Restarts. Hopes. Pages out 30 minutes later.

good.label: WITH CI/CD
good.html:
"It's in main? It's in prod."

Merging triggers the pipeline. Tests gate the deploy. Rollback is one click.

blocks.title: Under the hood — what a real pipeline does
blocks.html:
A typical pipeline: lint → unit tests → build artifact → push to a registry → deploy to staging → run integration tests against staging → (optional manual approval) → deploy to prod → smoke-test prod → notify a channel.

The exact tool — GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI, Jenkins — barely matters. What matters is that the same steps run the same way every time, and the green checkmark means something.

blocks.prompt: Last time something broke in production — could a pipeline step have caught it before it shipped?

lessons.title: Observability is a window, not a smoke alarm

sticky.html: Monitoring is a smoke alarm: it screams when the kitchen is already on fire. Observability is a glass wall: you can see what's cooking, all the time, before anything starts smelling.

blocks.html:
Observability is the broader idea. Logs, metrics, and traces — together — so you can ask new questions about a running system. Monitoring is what you set up after you know what to watch. Observability is what saves you when something breaks in a way nobody predicted.

If your only debugging tool in production is SSH and grep, welcome to the 1990s. Stay a while.
blocks.placeholder: metaphor: dashboard wall — graphs, logs, traces — engineer watching calmly with a coffee

blocks.label: THE THREE PILLARS
blocks.caption: different shapes of the same question: 'what is this system actually doing right now?'

items.question: What's the difference between a metric and a log line?
items.answer: A metric is a number you can graph: requests/sec, latency p99, error rate. A log line is a piece of text describing one event. Metrics scale cheaply. Logs are rich. You need both.

items.question: When is a trace more useful than a log?
items.answer: When a single request crosses many services. A trace shows you the whole journey — every hop, every span, where the 800ms went. Logs from each service alone can't reassemble that.

blocks.title: Under the hood — SLOs and 'good enough'
blocks.html:
You can't watch everything. Define a few SLOs (Service Level Objectives) — e.g. "99.5% of API requests under 300ms" — and watch those religiously. Everything else is interesting, but not paging-someone-at-3am important.

The honest secret: most outages are caught by the one alert tied to user-visible impact. Everything else is noise. Tune ruthlessly.

blocks.prompt: If your service died right now, what's the first place you'd look — and is that the fastest place to look?

outro.title: That's the DevOps field guide.
outro.subtitle: Now go automate the boring parts.

stickies.html:
✎ next volume idea:
"containers, kubernetes, and the difference between 'cloud-native' and 'wrote a Dockerfile.'"

✎ remember:
good DevOps is mostly reducing the number of things a human has to remember.
